dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing difficulty at Apt 210 in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Medic Bonnie. What made you call 911 today?
I've got trouble breathing. It's been getting worse since this morning.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where we are?
I'm Alice. We're in my apartment at 210.
And what day is it today?
Sunday, I think?
And what happened to you?
I can't breathe easily and I've been wheezing a lot.
When did you first notice the trouble breathing?
A couple of days ago with a cough, but it got worse this morning around 4 AM.
Are you coughing now or is there any pain?
No pain, just a lot of wheezing and I can't catch my breath.
We are going to check your breathing now. Are you okay with that?
Respirations at 28 per minute. Audible wheezing is heard.
Blood pressure is 150 over 100.
Pulse is 72, regular.
Peripheral pulses are strong and equal.
Skin appears warm and slightly pink.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
Pulse is 72, regular.
Respirations are 28.
Blood pressure is 150/100.
Blood glucose is 0-machine is non-functional, so no readout is recorded, per protocol.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
EKG is sinus rhythm, no abnormalities noted at this time.
Can you take a breath in for me so we can listen to the lungs?
Okay, I'll try.
Audible wheezing is present on both sides, primarily expiratory phase.
We're going to use 4 lead ECG monitoring now. No resistance reported from the patient.
We're going to give you 10 mg of Dexamethasone by mouth per standing order for respiratory distress and COPD exacerbation.
Okay, I'll take it now.
We are now applying nasal oxygen at 4 liters per minute. You may feel a bit of a cool sensation.
Pulse is 74 now, still regular.
Respirations have slowed to 21 per minute since oxygen was applied.
Blood pressure is 135/95, slightly lower now after monitoring for a few minutes.
Blood glucose machine remains non-functional-code 0 recorded again, per policy not to assume new data without working equipment.
Oxygen saturation is now at 99%, maintaining well on oxygen support.
EKG remains in normal sinus rhythm. Repeat ECG is part of the respiratory distress protocol now.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15, eyes open to speech, oriented to person, place, and time. No noticeable decline since initial check.
Other than the breathing and wheezing, how are you feeling now? Any dizziness or nausea added in the last 10 minutes?
No, just the same as before-no new issues yet. Still tight in the chest a bit, but nothing else added.
Do you have any allergies we need to note in your chart before proceeding further?
Yes, I'm allergic to penicillin and tomatoes.
What medications are you currently taking on a regular basis or as needed for your lungs or other issues?
I use Albuterol and Symbicort when needed for my breathing issues like this one.
Do you have a history of heart or lung issues, or anything else relevant to today's symptoms and treatment, like a smoking history or prior pneumonia?
I have COPD, asthma, and reactive airway, but no heart problems that I know of. I stopped smoking 10 years ago, maybe 12 years at this point since I had COPD diagnosed then too.
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink this morning before symptoms started to worsen?
I had some tea with my breakfast this morning-should have been before 8 AM, maybe around 6.
Can you tell me what was going on when you first noticed this trouble breathing getting out of control-were you exercising or resting when it started becoming a problem for you?

No, I was just trying to get up and get ready. I woke up with it, but I waited a while before calling because I thought maybe the Symbicort would help, but it didn't this time. So I called 911 around 10 something, I think the nurse said on the phone maybe it was early 10 or so as we were waiting for you guys.

On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you describe the difficulty in breathing you're having now-better than before, equal to, or worse now compared to when we first met you in the hall at the apartment building here today?

Let's see… I'd say an 8 still, even with the O2 and Dexamethasone we just did. It's definitely better than it was initially, but I still feel the tightness and the wheezing when I exhale, so I won't rate it any lower. I can manage with the O2 for now, so it's an 8, not a 10 anymore, and not a 1 or 2 either-that's how I rate it right now given the current condition of my breathing but no actual chest pain or abdominal pain as far as I can tell right now too.
Is it sharp, aching, or chest pressure, or is it something different that you might describe more clearly for us today as we work through your symptoms on our way to the hospital for more care there?

None of those. It's more of a tightness and wheeze when I exhale. I don't feel the chest pain or actual tightness in the sternum or anything like that. It feels more like congestion and wheezing again-that's the right words for how I see it myself today, so maybe I can say that the sensation is tightness in the airway is the better way to describe this thing that we're experiencing as uncomfortable, not painful, respiratory-wise in the normal ways people would think like chest or somewhere else-no pain reported, just big breathing effort for everything I try to do here now after starting out today with 4 in the morning and getting worse as the hours passed on the walk to the nurse or to the home in order to call 911 for help for this condition called COPD but with reactive airway features too, maybe also asthma as part of the overall diagnosis){

Based on your symptoms and medical history, I'm activating protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress, which includes asthma, COPD exacerbation, and reactive airway. Croup is excluded due to your age and the nature of your symptoms, which are chronic and not sudden viral in origin.

Let me retake your breathing. It appears it's still 21 per minute, slightly less compared to before the oxygen was administered-improving when matched with the 4 L oxygen at the mask-still a bit of wheeze but less now versus where we started with expiratory phase so the numbers are considered a slight better today that I think is what we can say here briefly?
Oxygen saturation is still 99%, consistent with the current intervention using nasal oxygen at 4 LPM.

EKG is in normal sinus rhythm, still activated as per protocol. Nothing new to report compared to initial EKG reading we had found inside minutes of arrival for the emergency call we are handling today.

Blood pressure is down to 135/95 now from the 150/100 we noted earlier-still within normal range with less than expected drop for an active airway disease such as COPD or asthma but nothing remarkable to add or flag in the records for this stage of care.

Has the breathing difficulty changed in intensity at all during the last 10-15 minutes, especially after we gave you the medication and started oxygen therapy, and is the feeling any better, worse, or the same as it was when you first noticed the problem in the morning after waking up at 4? )

No, maybe feels a bit better with the oxygen I guess. Still not 100% normal-but maybe 75% there. Just taking it as is, right now, in this moment with support from the team here in front of me today for this transport that I need soon to the facility so I can get more care, if you knew what I meant}

Would you say you feel any new pain anywhere else on your body or is the sensation the same as just tightness and wheezing, with no change to where it is focused today in your chest area-still no pain for us to record so that's really non-specific and smiled when I think about that on the page"];
"No pain anywhere else. I am only still focused on the breathing difficulty. So it's no new additions where I stayed-still all lungs, really, and no cardiac-side issues identified here so far." };

The rest of your physical exam shows no complaints beyond the respiratory ones, and we've ruled out acute pain sources related to any trauma or internal ailments like suspected stroke or heart conditions. Is there a specific hospital you've been prescribed to go to for these types of issues, like a long-standing preference, that we can follow here? ";
I would like to go to Henrico Doctors' Hospital, please. That's my choice today.";
We're clearing your transport to that location now. ";
We are now going to move you into the stretcher and transport you to the hospital in the safest way. Would you be okay with that?
That's fine with me.
We are securing the patient in a sitting position now. Let me know if that's comfortable.
We are ready for transport. Pulse 74, respiration 21, blood pressure 135/95. Transporting you in a cold mode as per your stable presentation and choice of hospital. ";
"We are transferring Ms. Alice to Henrico Doctors' Hospital with a field impression of COPD exacerbation, asthma, and reactive airway. Vital signs are stable en route.";
"Dexamethasone 10 mg PO administered via standing order. ECG results were normal, and oxygen has been administered. No IV access placed. Patient choice for destination_);
"Patient has no pain reported. Field impression excludes croup." ;
Care of the patient was transferred to hospital staff without incident. Examination showed normal findings beyond the respiratory distress,";
